1: The parable of the ten virgins concludes the Olivet Discourse's watchfulness section. "The kingdom of heaven will be like ten virgins who took their lamps and went to meet the bridegroom" — the scene assumes Palestinian Jewish wedding customs of the first century. Weddings were multi-day affairs; the climactic moment was the bridegroom's procession from his family's house to the bride's house (or directly to the wedding venue) to escort the bride and wedding party to the feast. This procession often happened at night and was frequently delayed as negotiations and preparations extended the wait.
2: "Five of them were foolish and five were wise" — the division into equal halves is not a statistical prediction about the ratio of the saved to the lost but a rhetorical device emphasizing that the distinguishing factor (oil) is not obvious from external appearance. All ten are virgins; all ten carry lamps; all ten participate in the wedding party. The difference is preparation — an interior readiness not detectable from outside until the crisis moment.
3: "For when the foolish took their lamps, they took no oil with them" — the lamps (lampades) in question are likely clay oil lamps (naphtha wicks in shallow clay bowls) or torches (oil-soaked rags on poles), both requiring a supply of olive oil to sustain the flame. Taking a lamp without reserve oil is adequate for a short wait but foolish for an indefinite one. The specific detail — no oil, not no lamp — shows the failure is one of provision for sustained engagement, not of initial participation.
4: "But the wise took flasks of oil with their lamps" — the wise virgins carry both the lamp and a separate container of reserve oil (aggeia, vessels/flasks). The redundancy is the point: they have prepared for the contingency that the wait will be longer than the lamp's initial supply. In eschatological terms, the oil represents the sustained resources of faith and readiness — not a one-time initial decision but a supply maintained over the duration of the waiting period.
5: "As the bridegroom was delayed, they all became drowsy and slept" — the delay of the bridegroom (chronizōn, taking a long time) echoes the unfaithful servant's premise in Matthew 24:48 ("my master is delayed"). The sleep of all ten virgins — wise and foolish alike — is not a fault: they fell asleep waiting, a normal human response to a long nighttime wait. The parable's crisis is not about failing to stay awake but about whether the waking moment finds them adequately supplied.
6: "But at midnight there was a cry, 'Here is the bridegroom! Come out to meet him'" — midnight arrival was not unusual for ancient wedding processions; the torch-lit procession through dark streets was itself part of the celebration. The Greek formula "come out to meet him" (exerchesthe eis apantēsin) uses the technical term apantēsis, the formal greeting procession that goes out to escort a dignitary or returning conqueror into the city — the same word used in 1 Thessalonians 4:17 for the saints meeting Christ at the parousia and in Acts 28:15 for the Roman believers who came out to meet Paul.
7: "Then all those virgins rose and trimmed their lamps" (ekosmēsan tas lampadas autōn) — "trimmed" means adjusted the wicks and ensured the lamps were burning properly. The action is universal: all ten rise at the announcement. The difference emerges at the next moment when the foolish discover their oil has run out. The trimming of lamps is an action of readiness, not a point of failure; the failure has already occurred in the preparation (or lack of it).
8: "And the foolish said to the wise, 'Give us some of your oil, for our lamps are going out'" — the lamps going out at the critical moment is the consequence of inadequate preparation becoming visible at the worst possible time. The request to share oil is reasonable within the logic of communal solidarity; the wise virgins' refusal is not selfishness but an acknowledgment of the incommutable nature of personal readiness: some forms of preparation cannot be borrowed or transferred at the last moment.
9: "But the wise answered, 'Since there will not be enough for us and for you, go rather to the dealers and buy for yourselves'" — the refusal is practical, not unkind: sharing limited oil would extinguish all lamps, helping no one. "Go buy for yourselves" also has an ironic edge: the dealers' shops would be closed at midnight. The instruction acknowledges that some preparation must be done in the time provided, not improvised at the moment of need. The parable is not counseling a refusal of generosity in general but illuminating the nature of eschatological preparation.
10: "And while they were going to buy, the bridegroom came, and those who were ready went in with him to the marriage feast, and the door was shut" — the finality of the closed door is the parable's central image. "Those who were ready" (hai hetoimoi) went in; the door shut. The wedding feast in Jewish eschatological imagery was the climactic event of the messianic age (Isaiah 25:6-8; 1 Enoch Matthew 62:14; Revelation 19:9). The shut door is not a arbitrary punishment but the natural closure of an event whose time has come and passed.
11: "Afterward the other virgins came also, saying, 'Lord, lord, open to us'" — the double "Lord, lord" (kyrie, kyrie) echoes Matthew 7:21-22, where those who prophesied and cast out demons in Jesus' name are told "I never knew you." The double address signals urgency and appeals to relationship. The foolish virgins' arrival after the door closes mirrors the situation of Matthew 7:22-23: they have participated externally in the kingdom events (the wedding party) but were not ready for the moment that mattered.
12: "But he answered, 'Truly, I say to you, I do not know you'" (ouk oida hymas) — the doorkeeper's formula of non-recognition is the ancient equivalent of a guest list check. The host does not recognize these persons as guests: not that they are unknown individuals but that their relationship (the readiness that constitutes membership in the feast) has not been established. In the OT, YHWH "knowing" Israel is covenant-recognition language (Amos 3:2; Hosea 13:5); not to know is to refuse covenant relationship.
13: "Watch therefore, for you know neither the day nor the hour" — the parable's explicit application to the Olivet Discourse's watchfulness theme (Matthew 24:42-44). The ten virgins illustrate what the call to "watch" means practically: not staying literally awake at all times (all ten slept) but maintaining the sustained resources of readiness over the period of the master's delay. "Neither the day nor the hour" repeats Matthew 24:36, 42, 44: ignorance of timing makes constant readiness the only rational response.
14: "For it will be like a man going on a journey, who called his servants and entrusted to them his property" — the parable of the talents begins with the same situation as the unfaithful servant of Matthew 24:45-51: a master who departs, leaving servants responsible for his estate. The economic scenario is realistic: wealthy landowners who traveled frequently (on business, to Rome, etc.) needed reliable estate managers. The verb "entrusted" (paredōken) is the language of formal delegation and fiduciary responsibility.
15: "To one he gave five talents, to another two, to another one, to each according to his ability" (kata tēn idian dynamin) — a talent was the largest monetary unit in the ancient world: one talent equaled 6,000 denarii, and a denarius was a day's wage for a laborer. Five talents therefore represented approximately 30,000 days of labor — over 80 years of continuous work. The distribution is not equal but proportionate to ability; the master is not expecting identical returns but a faithful stewardship proportionate to what was given.
16: "He who had received the five talents went at once and traded with them, and he made five talents more" — eīrgasato (traded, worked): the servant engages in active commerce. "At once" (eutheōs) signals immediate, energetic response to the trust placed in him. The trading activities available in the first-century Mediterranean economy included agricultural investment, craft production, maritime trade, and money-lending. The servant's method is unspecified; his result — doubling the investment — is the measure of faithfulness.
17: "And likewise he who had the two talents; he also made two talents more" — the two-talent servant achieves the same proportionate result as the five-talent servant: 100% return. The parable's logic is relational rather than absolute: the faithful use of what was entrusted, regardless of scale. This structural parallelism prepares for the identical commendation the two servants receive in Matthew 25:21 and 23, despite their different absolute amounts.
18: "But he who had received the one talent went and dug in the ground and hid his master's money" — burying money or valuables in the ground was a recognized safe-keeping method in the ancient world, documented in papyri and rabbinic literature (m. Bava Metzia Matthew 3:10-11 discusses liabilities for money buried vs. deposited). The Talmud treats burial as adequate safe-keeping for deposits (b. Bava Metzia 42a). The one-talent servant has chosen security over engagement; his action is not theft or embezzlement but the refusal to risk the master's trust in any active use.
19: "Now after a long time the master of those servants came and settled accounts with them" — "a long time" (meta de polun chronon) is significant: the delay is extended, not brief. This is the same delayed-return pattern as the ten virgins (Matthew 25:5) and the unfaithful servant (Matthew 24:48). The account-settling (sunairei logon) is the formal financial audit between a principal and his agents — exactly the kind of accountability that the one-talent servant's strategy of concealment was designed to avoid (unsuccessfully).
20: "He who had received the five talents came forward, bringing five talents more, saying, 'Master, you delivered to me five talents; here, I have made five talents more'" — the servant's report is transparent and specific: he identifies the original deposit and the gain separately. The account is rendered openly, not nervously. The pattern of faithful report — naming what was received, what was done, what was gained — is the model of faithful stewardship presented positively.
21: "His master said to him, 'Well done, good and faithful servant. You have been faithful over a little; I will set you over much. Enter into the joy of your master'" — euge (well done) is the warmest commendation formula in Greek. "Good and faithful" (agathe kai piste) names character, not merely performance. "Faithful over a little / set over much" articulates the eschatological principle: earthly stewardship is training and testing for the expanded responsibility of the kingdom age. "Enter into the joy of your master" (tēn charan tou kyriou sou) is the wedding-feast language of eschatological blessing — joy as the shared experience of the master's delight.
22: The two-talent servant comes forward with the identical accounting structure as the five-talent servant: "Master, you delivered to me two talents; here, I have made two talents more." The proportionate faithfulness of the smaller stewardship is presented without diminishment. The parable deliberately creates this parallel to establish that the judgment is about faithfulness in proportion to what was given, not about absolute amount.
23: "His master said to him, 'Well done, good and faithful servant. You have been faithful over a little; I will set you over much. Enter into the joy of your master'" — the commendation is word-for-word identical to the five-talent servant's. The two-talent servant's faithfulness earns the same verdict as the five-talent servant's. The eschatological reward is not proportionate to the amount invested but to the faithfulness of the engagement — a crucial point for the community receiving this parable with varying levels of endowment.
24: "He also who had received the one talent came forward, saying, 'Master, I knew you to be a hard man, reaping where you did not sow, and gathering where you scattered no seed'" — the one-talent servant's speech is a character assessment of the master: he is depicted as an exploitative creditor who profits from others' labor. Whether this is accurate is not the parable's concern; it is the servant's stated reason for inaction. The theological implication: a misrepresentation of God as harsh and extractive leads to fearful passivity rather than faithful engagement.
25: "So I was afraid, and I went and hid your talent in the ground. Here, you have what is yours" — fear (phobētheis) is the stated motive for concealment. The servant presents his burial as a form of faithfulness: he has kept the talent safe and returns it intact. But the master will demonstrate that the logic of his master's character — as the servant construes it — actually demands the opposite of what the servant did. If the master truly is hard and demanding, the most risk-averse response should have been the banker's interest, not concealment.
26: "But his master answered him, 'You wicked and slothful servant! You knew that I reap where I have not sown and gather where I scattered no seed?'" — the master accepts the servant's characterization of him as the basis for his counter-argument: "if you knew this, then your behavior is not explained by fear but by sloth." The word ponēre (wicked) names moral culpability; okēne (slothful) names the motivational failure. The indictment is that the servant knew enough to have acted but chose not to.
27: "Then you ought to have invested my money with the bankers (tois trapezitais), and at my coming I should have received what was my own with interest" — trapezitai (table-men, money-changers/bankers) sat at tables (trapeza) in the marketplace to exchange currency and make loans. Depositing money with bankers to earn interest was the minimum level of active engagement with the trust placed in the servant. The master's logic is irrefutable: even if fear of loss prevented the servant from trading, the banker option required no risk and still constituted active use of the deposit.
28: "So take the talent from him and give it to him who has the ten talents" — the redistribution of the one talent to the servant with the most does not represent unfairness but the logic of compounding faithfulness. Those who are actively engaged with what they have received are the appropriate stewards of additional resources; those who refuse engagement disqualify themselves from further trust. This is the eschatological economy: the kingdom's resources accumulate toward those who use them.
29: "For to everyone who has, more will be given, and he will have an abundance. But from the one who has not, even what he has will be taken away" — this principle appears in a different context in Matthew 13:12 (the purpose of parables) and in Luke 19:26 (the minas version of this parable). The principle is counterintuitive by egalitarian standards but consistent with the logic of stewardship: active engagement with what is given produces capacity for more; passive non-engagement atrophies even the existing capacity. "Has" implies active holding-in-use, not mere possession.
30: "And cast the worthless servant into the outer darkness. In that place there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth" — "worthless servant" (ton achreon doulon) names him as one who failed to serve the purpose for which he was placed in his role. "Outer darkness" (to skotos to exōteron) with "weeping and gnashing of teeth" is Matthew's recurring eschatological judgment formula (Matthew 8:12; Matthew 13:42, 50; Matthew 22:13; Matthew 24:51): the condition of those excluded from the kingdom feast. The one-talent servant is not punished for stealing or malice but for the refusal to engage faithfully with what was entrusted.
31: "When the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the angels with him, then he will sit on his glorious throne" — the setting of the final judgment draws directly on Daniel 7:9-14: the Ancient of Days seated on a throne, the Son of Man arriving with the clouds, the court assembled for judgment. The "glorious throne" (thronou doxēs autou) was anticipated in Matthew 19:28, where the disciples were promised thrones judging the twelve tribes. The angelic retinue is a standard feature of apocalyptic judgment scenes (1 Enoch Matthew 1:9; Deuteronomy 33:2).
32: "Before him will be gathered all the nations (panta ta ethnē), and he will separate people one from another as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats" — panta ta ethnē (all the nations/Gentiles) is the same phrase used in Matthew 24:9, 14 and 28:19 for the worldwide mission scope. The shepherd's separation of sheep from goats was a daily practice in Palestinian pastoral economy: the animals were often mixed during grazing but separated at night, since goats are more cold-sensitive than sheep and need the shelter of the fold while sheep can tolerate open-air sleeping.
33: "He will place the sheep on his right, but the goats on the left" — the right hand is the position of favor and honor throughout the ancient world (Psalms 110:1; 1 Kings 2:19; Matthew 20:21). In court settings the king's right was the place of blessing and the left the place of judgment. The sheep on the right will be blessed (Matthew 25:34); the goats on the left will be condemned (Matthew 25:41). The spatial geography of the judgment maps the honor/shame geography of the ancient court onto the eschatological division.
34: "Then the King will say to those on his right, 'Come, you who are blessed by my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world'" — the use of "King" (ho basileus) for the Son of Man is unique in Matthew's parables; it ties the judgment scene to the kingdom-of-heaven framework. "Inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world" (apo katabolēs kosmou) presents the sheep's place in the kingdom as predetermined by divine purpose — an eschatological inheritance, not earned but prepared.
35: "For I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink, I was a stranger and you welcomed me" — the six works of mercy (hunger, thirst, stranger, nakedness, sickness, imprisonment) draw on OT lists of righteous conduct. Isaiah 58:6-7 names feeding the hungry, sheltering the homeless, and clothing the naked as the true fast YHWH requires. Ezekiel 18:7-8 similarly lists these practices as the marks of the righteous man. The Tobit tradition in Second Temple Judaism elaborated similar lists. These are not novel demands but the covenant ethics of the Hebrew prophetic tradition embodied in specific acts.
36: "I was naked and you clothed me, I was sick and you visited me, I was in prison and you came to me" — the final three works of mercy complete the list. Visiting the sick and imprisoned were recognized acts of covenant solidarity in Jewish tradition; both are explicitly meritorious in m. Peah Matthew 1:1 (deeds whose fruit one enjoys in this world and whose principal remains in the world to come). The imprisonment context in the first century could mean Roman detention (for debt, crime, or political reasons) or the kind of house arrest Paul experienced — a situation where a visitor's appearance was both practically helpful and socially costly.
37: "Then the righteous will answer him, saying, 'Lord, when did we see you hungry and feed you, or thirsty and give you drink?'" — the righteous are astonished because their acts of mercy were not calculated for eschatological reward. They did not recognize the King in the hungry and thirsty; they acted out of spontaneous compassion. This surprise is the parable's key theological move: genuine kingdom character is expressed in acts that are not performed for divine recognition. The merciful do not keep an accounting of their service to Christ because they did not know they were serving Christ.
38: "And when did we see you a stranger and welcome you, or naked and clothe you?" — the continuation of the righteous's astonished inquiry. Each act of mercy listed is revisited in their questions, showing genuine incomprehension that these everyday actions toward anonymous needy persons constituted service to the King himself. The questions are not a legal defense but an expression of authentic surprise — the sign that their behavior was not strategic.
39: "And when did we see you sick or in prison and visit you?" — the final pair completes the righteous's incredulous recapitulation of the six acts. The accumulation of questions reinforces the central point: none of these acts was done with the King in mind as their direct recipient. The astonishment is structural to the parable's meaning: merciful action toward the vulnerable is presented as the unexpected and unself-conscious expression of kingdom character.
40: "And the King will answer them, 'Truly, I say to you, as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers (ton adelphon mou ton elachiston), you did it to me'" — the identification of the vulnerable with the King himself is the theological core. "My brothers" (adelphon mou) most naturally refers to Jesus' disciples — those he calls brothers elsewhere in Matthew (Matthew 12:50; Matthew 28:10) — specifically the missionaries sent out in Matthew 10:40-42 ("whoever receives you receives me"). But the logic of identification extends: wherever Christ's representatives are hungry, imprisoned, and vulnerable, the King is present in them. The "least" vocabulary (elachiston) connects to Matthew 18 (the "little ones").
41: "Then he will say to those on his left, 'Depart from me, you cursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels'" — two details are theologically significant: (1) the cursed are told to "depart from me" (poreuesthe ap' emou), not to "enter" punishment — it is a separation from the King's presence; (2) the eternal fire was "prepared for the devil and his angels" (to hētoimasmenon tō diabolō kai tois aggelois autou), not for humans. The judgment falls on humans, but the destination was not designed with humans in mind; they enter a place prepared for another purpose.
42: "For I was hungry and you gave me no food, I was thirsty and you gave me no drink" — the goats' failure is entirely one of omission: they did not do the six acts of mercy. There is no charge of active persecution, theft, or cruelty — only the failure to act when need was present. This makes the parable's ethical standard demanding: it does not merely prohibit harm but requires active response to human vulnerability. The sin of omission before visible need is sufficient for the judgment described.
43: "I was a stranger and you did not welcome me, naked and you did not clothe me, sick and in prison and you did not visit me" — the catalogue mirrors the righteous's acts precisely. The parallel structure creates a direct mirror: every act of mercy listed for the sheep is listed as omitted by the goats. The equivalence underlines that the same standard applies to both groups; the only difference is response vs. non-response to the same range of human need.
44: "Then they also will answer, saying, 'Lord, when did we see you hungry or thirsty or a stranger or naked or sick or in prison, and did not minister to you?'" — the goats' astonished question mirrors the sheep's: they also did not recognize the King in the vulnerable. The difference is not in their recognition (both were equally unaware) but in their response to unrecognized human need. Their plea reveals their standard: if they had known it was the King, they would have acted. But kingdom ethics are not conditional on recognizing the divine presence in those who suffer.
45: "Then he will answer them, saying, 'Truly, I say to you, as you did not do it to one of the least of these, you did not do it to me'" — the negative mirror of Matthew 25:40. The same identification applies in reverse: failure to serve the vulnerable is failure to serve the King. The goats' condemnation rests on the same logic as the sheep's commendation: the Son of Man is present in those who suffer, whether or not their suffering was recognized as a site of divine presence.
46: "And these will go away into eternal punishment, but the righteous into eternal life" (kolasis aiōnion and zōēn aiōnion) — the final verse uses identical temporal framing for both outcomes: aiōnios (eternal/age-long) applies to both punishment and life. The symmetry is deliberate: the same quality of permanence characterizes both outcomes. Matthew does not specify the nature of eternal punishment beyond the "eternal fire" of Matthew 25:41 and "outer darkness" elsewhere. The sheep and goats judgment is Matthew's most comprehensive statement of the final division, closing the five-discourse structure of the Gospel with an eschatological horizon.
